UX / UI · Design Systems · Brand Identity

Design System for
Zoo Magazine

A comprehensive design system built for Zoo Magazine's digital platform — establishing visual language, component libraries, spacing logic, and interaction patterns for easy understanding, implementation, and long-term maintenance of website and app interfaces.

Client

Zoo Magazine — Amsterdam

Role

Graphic & UX Designer

Year

2023

Tools

Figma · Adobe XD

Scope

UX / UI Design System Brand Identity
🔒

Privacy note: I designed the full landing page and navigation pages for Zoo Magazine's website. As the site is not yet live and the client retains ownership of the final designs, the full UI is not shared here. What is shown below is the Design System — the visual foundation I built to ensure consistent, scalable experiences across the platform.

01 — Design System Overview

A visual language built for
consistency and scale

Zoo Magazine is a high-end Amsterdam fashion publication with a discerning audience and a bold editorial identity. To bring this identity into the digital space, I developed a full design system in Figma — giving the internal team and future developers a single source of truth for every design decision.

The system was designed to be both comprehensive and approachable — structured enough to enforce visual consistency, yet flexible enough to accommodate the magazine's evolving editorial content.

Colour Palette — A curated set of brand and functional colours ensuring accessibility and visual hierarchy across all pages.

Typography System — Type scale, font pairings, and usage rules that reflect the magazine's editorial voice in digital form.

Component Library — Reusable UI elements covering action, communication, containment, navigation, selection, and text input.

Spacing & Layout Grid — A purposeful spatial system where every positive and negative space is intentionally defined.

Client feedback

"

She gets inside your head.
She understands how you see
your magazine.

— Editor, Zoo Magazine Amsterdam

Working with a high-end fashion publication means understanding an aesthetic sensibility that goes far beyond a brief. Zoo Magazine's editor-in-chief noted that I was able to translate his vision for the magazine — its tone, its luxury positioning, its audience — into design decisions without him having to spell it out.

That instinct — to deeply understand the perspective behind a project before touching a single element — is one of the qualities I bring to every client relationship, particularly across language and cultural contexts.

02 — Colour Palette & Typography

The aesthetic foundation of
every design decision

Colour and typography are the two pillars that determine the overall aesthetic of the system. For Zoo Magazine, the palette was drawn from the brand's editorial history — refined, bold, and rooted in fashion-forward sensibility. The type system pairs editorial serif display faces with clean, legible sans-serifs for body text, creating a balance between luxury feel and digital readability.

Design principle: Colour palette and typography determine the overall aesthetic of the design system — every subsequent decision flows from these foundations.

03 — Components & Patterns

Interactive elements that
drive the experience

Elements which react, respond, or navigate when clicked or hovered are the key components and patterns of the system. They define the building blocks of action, communication, containment, navigation, selection, and text input — covering every mode of interaction a reader might have with the platform.

Each component was documented with its default, hover, active, and disabled states, giving developers a clear specification without ambiguity.

Two components worth highlighting: a contextual search bar designed against a full black canvas — anchored by the iconic Berlin Tower footer, Zoo Magazine's symbolic connection to its founding city — creates a focused, immersive search experience true to the brand. Alongside it, a fixed quick-navigation button persists on screen at all times, ensuring readers can move fluidly through sections without ever losing their place — a small detail that makes a significant difference in editorial UX.

04 — Spacing & Layout

Space as a design element —
intentional and purposeful

All positive and negative space in the layout is purposely planned to add value to each and every element in the design. The spatial system is built on a consistent base unit, ensuring that whitespace is never an afterthought — it's a deliberate part of the visual hierarchy.

This approach gives Zoo Magazine's digital platform the editorial breathing room its audience expects from a premium fashion publication.

05 — Navigation & User Flows

Designed for smooth,
intuitive journeys

The goal of the design system is to facilitate easy navigation for the best understanding, implementation, and maintenance of the interface — creating a user experience that is smooth and effortless. Navigation patterns were designed to guide readers through editorial content naturally, without friction, reflecting the seamless experience expected of a premium digital publication.

The outcome

"By building a structured design system from the ground up, I gave Zoo Magazine's team a shared visual language — one that ensures their digital presence will remain consistent, scalable, and true to the brand as the platform grows."
